# C codebase enhancements for distributed document system (2025-11-04 21:22Z)

_**User (2025-11-04 21:26Z)**_

#codebase are all these functions being checked, check horouly, 


You said:
The system is composed of the following core components:

User Clients:
Represent the users interacting with the system.
Provide the interface for performing operations on files (create, view, read, write, delete, etc.).
Multiple clients may run concurrently, and all must be able to interact with the system simultaneously.
Name Server:
Acts as the central coordinator of the system.
Handles all communication between clients and storage servers.
Maintains the mapping between file names and their storage locations.
Ensures efficient and correct access to files across the system.
Storage Servers:
Responsible for storing and retrieving file data.
Ensure durability, persistence, and efficient access to files.
Support concurrent access by multiple clients, including both reads and writes.
At any point, there would be a single instance of the Name Server running, to which multiple instances of Storage Servers and User Clients can connect. The User Clients and Storage Servers can disconnect and reconnect at any time, and the system should handle these events gracefully.

The event of Name Server failure is out of scope for this project. That is, if the Name Server goes down, the entire system is considered down and must be restarted.

The File
Files are the fundamental units of data in the system, each uniquely identified by a name. Files are restricted to text data only. Every file consists of multiple sentences, and each sentence is made up of words. A sentence is defined as a sequence of words ending with a period (.), exclamation mark (!), or question mark (?). Words within a sentence are separated by spaces. This segmentation needs to be handled by the system, the user should be able to access the file as a whole. For completeness and in interest of no ambiguity, a word is defined as a sequence of ASCII characters without spaces.

There is no imposed limit on file size or the total number of files, so the system must efficiently handle both small and large documents (which can variably grow after creation also).

Files support concurrent access for both reading and writing. However, when a user edits a sentence, that sentence is locked for editing by others until the operation is complete. This allows multiple users to view or edit the file simultaneously, but prevents simultaneous edits to the same sentence.

[150] User Functionalities
The users (clients) must be able to perform the following operations:

[10] View files: User can view all files they have access to. They can view all files on the system, irrespective of the access using “-a” flag. “-l” flag should list files along with details like word count, character count, last access, owner, etc. Note, a combination of flags can be used lke “-al”, which should list all the files with details.
VIEW  # Lists all files user has access to
VIEW -a # Lists all files on the system
VIEW -l # Lists all user-access files with details
VIEW -al # Lists all system files with details
[10] Read a File: Users can retrieve the contents of files stored within the system. This fundamental operation grants users access to the information they seek.
READ <filename> # Prints the content of the complete file
[10] Create a File: Users can create new files, allowing them to store and manage their data effectively.
CREATE <filename> # Creates an empty file with name <filename>
[30] Write to a File: Users can update the content of the file at a word level. This operation allows users to modify and append data to existing files.
WRITE <filename> <sentence_number>  # Locks the sentence for other users (if no file write access, should return appropriate here)
<word_index> <content> # Updates the sentence at <word_index> with <content>
.
.
.
<word_index> <content> # User can update the sentence multiple times
ETIRW # Relieves the sentence lock, allowing other users to finally write
Few important points to note here:

After each WRITE completion, the sentence index update. So, care must be taken for ensuring concurrent WRITEs are handled correctly.
The content may contain characters like period (.), exclamation mark (!), or question mark (?). The system should be able to recognise these sentence delimiters and create seperate sentences accordingly. (Please refer to examples given below, for more clarity).
Yes, every period (or question / exclamation mark) is a sentence delimiter, even if it is in the middle of a word like “e.g.” or “Umm… ackchually!”
Hint: For resolving concurrent read-write issues, you may write to a temporary swap file initially, and move the contents to the final file once all updates are complete. You may also consider using locks, semaphores, some algorithmic approach, etc.

[15] Undo Change: Users can rever the last changes made to a file.
UNDO <filename> # Reverts the last change made to the file
Note: The undo-es are file specific, and not user specific. So, if user A makes a change, and user B wants to undo it, user B can also do it. The undo history is maintained by the storage server.

[10] Get Additional Information: Users can access a wealth of supplementary information about specific files. This includes details such as file size, access rights, timestamps, and other metadata, providing users with comprehensive insights into the files they interact with.
INFO <filename> # Display details in any convenient format, just that all above-mentioned details should be there
[10] Delete a File: Owners should be able to remove files from the system when they are no longer needed, contributing to efficient space management. All data like user access should be accordingly updated to reflect this change.
DELETE <filename> # Deletes the file <filename>
[15] Stream Content: The client establishes direct connection with the Storage Server and fetches & displays the content word-by-word with a delay of 0.1 seconds between each word. This simulates a streaming effect, allowing users to experience the content in a dynamic manner.
STREAM <filename> # Streams the content of the file word by word with a delay of 0.1 seconds
Note: If the storage server goes down mid-streaming, an appropriate error message should be displayed to the user.

[10] List Users: Users can view a list of all users registered in the system.
LIST # Lists all users in the system
[15] Access: The creator (owner) of the file can provide access to other users. The owner can provide read or write access. The owner can also remove access from other users. The owner always has both read and write access.
ADDACCESS -R <filename> <username>  # Adds read access to the user
ADDACCESS -W <filename> <username>  # Adds write (and read) access to the user
REMACCESS <filename> <username>  # Removes all access
[15] Executable File: Users (with read access) can “execute” the file. Execute, here, means executing the file content as shell commands. The output of the command should be displayed to the user.
EXEC <filename> # Executes the file content as shell commands
Note: The execution must happen on the name server; and the outputs as is should be piped to the client interface

[40] System Requirements
The system must support the following requirements:

[10] Data Persistence: All files and their associated metadata (like access control lists) must be stored persistently. This ensures that data remains intact and accessible even after Storage Servers restart or fail.

[5] Access Control: The system must enforce access control policies, ensuring that only authorized users can read or write to files based on the permissions set by the file owner.

[5] Logging: Implement a logging mechanism where the NM and SS records every request, acknowledgment and response. Additionally, the NM should display (print in terminal) relevant messages indicating the status and outcome of each operation. This bookkeeping ensures traceability and aids in debugging and system monitoring. Each entry should include relevant information such as timestamps, IP, port, usernames and other important operation details crucial for diagnosing and troubleshooting issues.

[5] Error Handling: The system must provide clear and informative error messages for all sorts of expected / unexpected failures, including interactions between clients, Name Server (NM), and Storage Servers (SS). Define a comprehensive set of error codes to cover scenarios such as unauthorized access, file not found, resource contention (e.g., file locked for writing) and system failures. These error codes should be universal throughout the system.

[15] Efficient Search: The Name Server should implement efficient search algorithms to quickly locate files based on their names or other metadata, minimizing latency in file access operations. Furthermore, caching should be implemented for recent searches to expedite subsequent requests for the same data.

Note: An approach faster than O(N) time complexity is expected here. Efficient data structures like Tries, Hashmaps, etc. can be used.

[10] Specifications
1. Initialisation
Name Server (NM): The first step is to initialize the Naming Server, which serves as the central coordination point in the NFS. It is responsible for managing the essential information about file locations and content.
Note: The IP address and port of the Naming Server can be assumed to be known publicly so that it can be provided to Clients and Storage servers while registering.

Storage Server (SS): Each Storage Server is responsible for physically storing the files and interacting with the Naming Server.Upon initialization, the SS sends vital details about its existence to the Naming Server. This information includes: IP address, port for NM connection, port for client connection and a list of files on it.

Client: Clients on initialisation should ask the user for their username (for file accesses) and pass this information along with its IP, NM port and SS port to the Name Server.

2. Name Server
Storing Storage Server data: One of the fundamental functions of the NM is to serve as the central repository for critical information provided by Storage Servers (SS) upon connection. This information is maintained by NM, to later direct data requests to appropriate storage server. As mentioned in specification 2, these lookups need to be efficient.
Client task feedback: Upon completion of tasks initiated by clients, the NM plays a pivotal role in providing timely and relevant feedback to the requesting clients. This is really important in real-systems where client response latency is pivotal.
3. Storage Servers
The Storage Servers are equipped with the following functionalities:

Adding new storage servers: New Storage Servers (i.e., which begin running after the initial initialisation phase) have the capability to dynamically add their entries to the NM at any point during execution. This flexibility ensures that the system can adapt to changes and scaling requirements seamlessly. The initialisation process at the storage server side follows the same protocol as described in Specification 1.
Commands Issued by NM: The Name Server can issue specific commands to the Storage Servers, such as creating, editing or deleting files. The Storage Servers are responsible for executing these commands as directed by the NM.
Client Interactions: Some operations require the client to establish direct connection with the storage server. The storage server is expected to facilitate these interactions as needed.
4. Client
Whenever a client boots up, it asks the user for their username. This username is then used for all file access control operations. The system should ensure that users can only perform actions on files they have permissions for, based on their username. This username is relayed to the NM, which stores it along with the client information until the client disconnects.

Clients initiate communication with the NM to interact with the system. Here’s how this interaction unfolds:

Any file access request from the client is first sent to the NM, which locates the corresponding Storage Server hosting that file (one of many), using its locally stored information.
Depending on the type of operation requested by the client, the NM may either handle the request as a middleman or facilitate direct communication between the client and the appropriate Storage Server. The operations can be broadly categorized as follows:
Reading, Writing, Streaming : The NM identifies the correct Storage Server and returns the precise IP address and client port for that SS to the client. Subsequently, the client directly communicates with the designated SS. This direct communication is established, and the client continuously receives information packets from the SS until a predefined “STOP” packet is sent or a specified condition for task completion is met. The “STOP” packet serves as a signal to conclude the operation.
Listing files, Basic Info and Access Control : The NM handles these requests directly. It processes the client’s request and retrieves the necessary information from its local storage. Once the information is gathered, the NM sends it back to the client, providing the requested details without involving any Storage Server.
Creating and Deleting Files : The NM determines the appropriate SS and forwards the request to the appropriate SS for execution. The SS processes the request and performs the specified action, such as creating / deleting the file. After successful execution, the SS sends an acknowledgment (ACK) to the NM to confirm task completion. The NM, in turn, conveys this information back to the client, providing feedback on the task’s status.
Execute : The NM requests for information from SS, but the main processing and communication is handled by the NM directly. The NM executes the commands contained within the file and captures the output. This output is then relayed back to the client, providing them with the results of the executed commands.
[50] Bonus Functionalities (Optional)
[10] Hierarchical Folder Structure: Allow users to create folders and subfolders to organize files. Users should be able to navigate through this hierarchy when performing file operations. Some associated commands that are expected to be implemented are:
CREATEFOLDER <foldername> # Creates a new folder
MOVE <filename> <foldername> # Moves the file to the specified folder
VIEWFOLDER <foldername> # Lists all files in the specified folder
[15] Checkpoints: Implement a checkpointing mechanism that allows users to save the state of a file at specific points in time. Users should be able to revert to these checkpoints if needed. The following commands are expected to be implemented:
CHECKPOINT <filename> <checkpoint_tag> # Creates a checkpoint with the given tag
VIEWCHECKPOINT <filename> <checkpoint_tag> # Views the content of the specified checkpoint
REVERT <filename> <checkpoint_tag> # Reverts the file to the specified checkpoint
LISTCHECKPOINTS <filename> # Lists all checkpoints for the specified file
[5] Requesting Access: Users can request access to files they do not own. The owner of the file can then approve or deny these requests. There is no need of a push-notification mechanism, a simple storing of requests and an owner-side feature to view and approve/reject requests is sufficient.

[15] Fault Tolerance: To ensure the robustness and reliability of the system, the following fault tolerance and data replication strategies need to be implemented:
Replication : Implement a replication strategy for data stored within the system. This strategy involves duplicating every file and folder in an SS in another SS. In the event of an SS failure, the NM should be able to retrieve the requested data from one of the replicated stores. Every write command should be duplicated asynchronously across all replicated stores. The NM does not wait for acknowledgment but ensures that data is redundantly stored for fault tolerance.
Failure Detection : The NM should be equipped to detect SS failures. This ensures that the system can respond promptly to any disruptions in SS availability.
SS Recovery : When an SS comes back online (reconnects to the NM), the duplicated stores should be matched back to the original SS. This ensures that the SS is synchronized with the current state of the system and can resume its role in data storage and retrieval seamlessly.
[5] The Unique Factor: What sets your implementation apart from others? Welp, this is where you showcase your innovation and creativity.
Examples
Note: There is no specification on the exact format of the commands. The commands mentioned in the examples are indicative. You may choose to implement them in any format you like, as long as the functionality remains the same.

Example 1: View File
Client: VIEW  # Lists files accessible to the user
--> wowee.txt
--> nuh_uh.txt

Client: VIEW -a  # Lists all files on the system
--> wowee.txt
--> nuh_uh.txt
--> grades.txt

Client: VIEW -l  # Lists files accessible to the user with details
---------------------------------------------------------
|  Filename  | Words | Chars | Last Access Time | Owner |
|------------|-------|-------|------------------|-------|
| wowee.txt  |   69  |  420  | 2025-10-10 14:32 | user1 |
| nuh_uh.txt |   37  |  123  | 2025-10-10 14:32 | user1 |
---------------------------------------------------------

Client: VIEW -al
---------------------------------------------------------
|  Filename  | Words | Chars | Last Access Time | Owner |
|------------|-------|-------|------------------|-------|
| wowee.txt  |   69  |  420  | 2025-10-10 14:32 | user1 |
| nuh_uh.txt |   37  |  123  | 2025-10-10 14:32 | user1 |
| grades.txt |   51  |  273  | 2025-10-10 14:32 | kaevi |
---------------------------------------------------------
Example 2: Read File
Client: READ wowee.txt  # Displays the content of the file
OSN assignments are so fun!

I love doing them.          Wish we had more of them.
Example 3: Create File
Client: CREATE mouse.txt  # Creates an empty file named mouse.txt
File Created Successfully!

Client: VIEW
--> wowee.txt
--> nuh_uh.txt
--> mouse.txt
Note: NS dynamically adds the new file to the list of available files and updates backup SSs. If the file already exists, NS responds with an appropriate error.

Example 4: Write to a File
Client: WRITE mouse.txt 0 # Adding to the start of file
Client: 1 Im just a mouse.
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Client: READ mouse.txt
Im just a mouse.

Client: WRITE mouse.txt 1  # In essence, appending to the file
Client: 1 I dont like PNS
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Client: READ mouse.txt
Im just a mouse. I dont like PNS

Client WRITE mouse.txt 2  # Caveat, note the lack of delimiter after last sentence. There are only 2 sentences.
ERROR: Sentence index out of range.  # Similarly for word indexes (negative or > number of words + 1, should results in errors)

Client: WRITE mouse.txt 1  # Inserting into the second sentence
Client: 3 T-T
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Client: READ mouse.txt
Im just a mouse. I dont like T-T PNS

Client: WRITE mouse.txt 0  # Inserting multiple times into a sentence
Client: 4 deeply mistaken hollow lil gei-fwen # New sentence : Im just a deeply mistaken hollow lil gei-fwen pocket-sized mouse.
Client: 6 pocket-sized  # New sentence : Im just a deeply mistaken hollow pocket-sized lil gei-fwen mouse.
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Client: READ mouse.txt
Im just a deeply mistaken hollow pocket-sized lil gei-fwen mouse. I dont like T-T PNS

Client: WRITE mouse.txt 1  # Inserting a sentence delimiter
Client: 5 and AAD. aaaah # New sentences : [I dont like T-T PNS and AAD.]* [aaaah]. Currently active status remains with the index at index 1
Client: 0 But,  # New sentence : [But, I dont like T-T PNS and AAD.]* [aaaah].
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Client: READ mouse.txt
Im just a deeply mistaken hollow pocket-sized lil gei-fwen mouse. But, I dont like T-T PNS and AAD. aaaah.
The multiples writes within a single WRITE call, are all considered a single operation. Note this while implementing features like UNDO

Proper error handling should cover:

Attempting to write without access
Attempting to write a locked sentence
Invalid indices
Updates are applied in order received, so later updates operate on the already modified sentence.
Example 5: Undo Change
Client: READ nuh uh.txt
rm -rf /  # Oops!

Client: WRITE nuh_uh.txt 0
Client: 0 sudo
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Client: READ nuh_uh.txt
sudo rm -rf /

Client: UNDO nuh_uh.txt
Undo Successful!

Client: READ nuh_uh.txt
rm -rf /
Note:

Only one undo operation for a file needs to be supported. Multiple undos are beyond the scope of this project.
Undo operates at the Storage Server level and only reverts the most recent change.
If the current user is user1 and the most recent modification to the file was made by user2, then an UNDO command issued by user1 should revert user2’s last change.
Example 6: Get Additional Information
Client: INFO nuh_uh.txt
--> File: feedback.txt
--> Owner: user1
--> Created: 2025-10-10 14:21
--> Last Modified: 2025-10-10 14:32
--> Size: 52 bytes
--> Access: user1 (RW)
--> Last Accessed: 2025-10-10 14:32 by user1
Example 7: Delete a File
Client: VIEW
--> wowee.txt
--> nuh_uh.txt
--> mouse.txt

Client: DELETE mouse.txt
File 'mouse.txt' deleted successfully!

Client: VIEW
--> wowee.txt
--> nuh_uh.txt
Example 8: Stream File
Same as READ, but the client receives the file content word-by-word (from the storage server) with a delay of 0.1 seconds between each word.

Example 9: List Users
Client: LIST
--> user1
--> user2
--> kaevi
Example 10: Access Control
Client: INFO nuh_uh.txt
--> File: feedback.txt
--> Owner: user1
--> Created: 2025-10-10 14:21
--> Last Modified: 2025-10-10 14:32
--> Size: 52 bytes
--> Access: user1 (RW)
--> Last Accessed: 2025-10-10 14:32 by user1

Client: ADDACCESS -R nuh_uh.txt user2
Access granted successfully!

Client: INFO nuh_uh.txt
--> File: feedback.txt
--> Owner: user1
--> Created: 2025-10-10 14:21
--> Last Modified: 2025-10-10 14:32
--> Size: 52 bytes
--> Access: user1 (RW), user2 (R)
--> Last Accessed: 2025-10-10 14:32 by user1

Client: ADDACCESS -W nuh_uh.txt user2
Access granted successfully!

--> File: feedback.txt
--> Owner: user1
--> Created: 2025-10-10 14:21
--> Last Modified: 2025-10-10 14:32
--> Size: 52 bytes
--> Access: user1 (RW), user2 (RW)
--> Last Accessed: 2025-10-10 14:32 by user1

Client: REMACCESS nuh_uh.txt user2
Access removed successfully!

Client: INFO nuh_uh.txt
--> File: feedback.txt
--> Owner: user1
--> Created: 2025-10-10 14:21
--> Last Modified: 2025-10-10 14:32
--> Size: 52 bytes
--> Access: user1 (RW)
--> Last Accessed: 2025-10-10 14:32 by user1
Example 11: Execute File
Suppose the content of LMAAO.txt is as follows:

echo "Running diagnostics..."
ls
echo "Done!"

Running siagnostics...
--> LMAAO.txt
--> RotT.txt
--> AUG.txt

WRITE A PROMPT, SATING EXACLTY WHAT WE NEED TO DO, WHAT SHOULD BE THE INPUT OUTPUT FORMAT, WHICH VSCODE COPILOT CGPT-5 WOULD BE ABLE TO DO IN 1 GO, 
ChatGPT said:

Thought for 13s
Prompt for VSCode Copilot / GPT-5 — Implement the mini NFS (NameServer + StorageServer + Client)
Goal (one-shot): produce a working, end-to-end prototype of the described system in one go. Deliver three runnable Python programs (single-file each): nm_server.py (Name Server), ss_server.py (Storage Server), and client.py (CLI client). They should talk over TCP using JSON messages and implement the full required command set (VIEW, READ, CREATE, WRITE, UNDO, INFO, DELETE, STREAM, LIST, ADDACCESS, REMACCESS, EXEC), sentence-level locking, persistent storage, basic access control, logging, and clear error codes. Keep code readable and commented; include a README block at top of each file explaining how to run and test.

Below is the exact specification you must implement and the input/output formats the programs must use. This prompt is tuned so Copilot / GPT-5 can generate the three files and a quick test script in one run.

High level behaviors (must be implemented)
One Name Server (NM) process — single instance only. NM stores metadata, user list, file → StorageServer mapping, ACLs, caches, and handles commands described below.

Multiple Storage Servers (SS) — each stores files on disk and maintains undo history (last version). On startup each SS registers with NM, providing its IP/port and file list.

Clients (CLI) connect to NM. Clients send commands to NM. For read/write/stream the NM returns SS address so client connects directly to SS for content transfer. For LIST/VIEW/INFO/ADDACCESS/REMACCESS/NM-handled commands, client talks only with NM.

Persistent storage: SS persists files and metadata (ACLs, owner, timestamps, undo backup) to disk (e.g., a folder per SS and JSON metadata files).

Sentence parsing: a sentence is any sequence of words ending at ., !, or ?. Words are ASCII sequences separated by spaces. Every occurrence of ., !, ? is a sentence delimiter (even in e.g.).

Sentence locking: when a client issues WRITE <file> <sentence_index> the target sentence is locked on that SS for the duration of the WRITE session. Other write attempts to the same sentence receive a ERR_LOCKED error.

Undo: only one level of undo per file (SS keeps last committed copy). UNDO reverts to previous version.

Streaming: SS will stream words with 0.1s delay to client over the client-SS connection. If SS disconnects mid-stream, client prints an error message.

EXEC: NM reads the file content from the SS and executes the content as shell commands on NM (using subprocess) and sends combined stdout/stderr back to client.

Simple replication (optional but prefer): NM maintains a list of SS replicas; on CREATE/WRITE/DELETE NM instructs at least one backup SS to copy the file asynchronously (fire-and-forget). The system must still function if a replica is down (use primary/secondary mapping).

Network / Message formats (JSON over TCP)
All messages are JSON objects, newline (\n) terminated. Responses are JSON too.

Client → NM (examples)
Registration (client startup):
{"type":"register_client","username":"alice","client_port":6001}

View files (flags optional):
{"type":"view","username":"alice","flags":"-al"}

Read file:
{"type":"read","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt"}

Create file:
{"type":"create","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt"}

Request write (NM will return the SS host:port and a write-token):
{"type":"request_write","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt","sentence_index":1}

Other NM commands: info, delete, list, addaccess, remaccess, undo, exec with similar fields.

NM → Client responses
A uniform response object:

Success example:
{"status":"OK","op":"view","payload":{...}}

Error:
{"status":"ERR","code":"ERR_NO_ACCESS","message":"User alice has no write access to foo.txt"}

Important error codes (must be used consistently):

ERR_NO_SUCH_FILE, ERR_NO_ACCESS, ERR_LOCKED, ERR_INVALID_INDEX, ERR_SS_DOWN, ERR_UNDO_EMPTY, ERR_ALREADY_EXISTS, ERR_INVALID_CMD, ERR_INTERNAL.

Client ↔ SS (after NM gives SS address)
When NM approves a read/write/stream, it returns {"status":"OK","op":"request_write","ss":{"host":"127.0.0.1","port":7001},"token":"abc123"}.

Client connects to SS and sends JSON commands:

Read content:
{"type":"read","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt","token":"abc123"} → SS streams full file in one JSON: {"status":"OK","content":"full text here"} or for stream mode SS sends a stream of JSON messages: {"type":"word","word":"Hello"} repeated and finally {"type":"stop"}.

Write session flow:

Client: {"type":"begin_write","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt","sentence_index":1,"token":"abc123"}
SS replies {"status":"OK","lock_id":"L123"} or {"status":"ERR","code":"ERR_LOCKED"...}

Client sends update lines as JSON (multiple)
{"type":"update","word_index":4,"content":"newword"}
SS replies {"status":"OK"} after each update.

Client ends with:
{"type":"end_write","lock_id":"L123","commit":true}
SS commits changes persistently, stores undo backup (previous file copy), updates metadata, replicates asynchronously and replies {"status":"OK","message":"Write Successful"}.

Undo:
{"type":"undo","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt"} → {"status":"OK","message":"Undo Successful"} or error.

File & metadata layout on Storage Server disk (exact)
Inside SS root folder (configurable) create:

files/ — each file stored as <filename>.txt (plain text).

meta/<filename>.json — JSON containing:

{
  "owner": "alice",
  "acl": {"alice":"RW","bob":"R"},
  "created": "2025-10-10T14:21:00",
  "last_modified": "2025-10-10T14:32:00",
  "last_accessed": "2025-10-10T14:32:00",
  "word_count": 123,
  "char_count": 456
}
undo/<filename>.bak — previous committed version (text).

log.txt — SS local operations log.

NM stores persistent metadata in nm_state.json including file→SS mapping, cached name trie/hashmap for quick lookup, user list, ACL master copy.

Sentence parsing algorithm (exact)
Implement a deterministic parser:

Split file into sentences by scanning text; every ., !, ? is considered the end of a sentence.

A sentence includes all characters from previous delimiter (or start) up to and including the delimiter.

Within a sentence, words are sentence.strip().split(" ") (consecutive spaces produce empty tokens—strip them).

Sentence indexes are 0-based for commands.

Edge cases:

If a user writes content containing delimiters, the write must re-scan and update sentence boundaries accordingly. After each WRITE commit, update sentence indices for subsequent writes.

Concurrency & locking (exact)
SS maintains locks: Dict[file -> Dict[sentence_index -> lock_info]]. lock_info includes lock_id, username, timestamp.

begin_write acquires the lock atomically (use thread lock or asyncio Lock). If lock exists return ERR_LOCKED.

While a lock is held only that client may update that sentence. Multiple sentences can be locked concurrently by different users.

When end_write is received with commit:true, SS replaces the text in files/<filename>.txt atomically (write to temp then os.replace), update metadata and undo/<filename>.bak with previous version.

Logging & Error handling
Every NM and SS must log JSON lines with timestamp, IP, port, username, op, args, status, and error code if any.

Define logging function used across servers.

Use clear error messages and codes from the list above.

Performance / search requirement
NM must implement an O(1) (average) lookup for filename → SS mapping using a hashmap. Additionally implement a simple trie for prefix searches (VIEW -a with filters if requested). Provide a small LRU cache for recent filename → SS lookups.

Deliverables (what to output)
Copilot / GPT-5 should generate:

nm_server.py — listens on TCP port; accepts client registration and NM-handled commands, provides SS allocation and returns SS addresses for reads/writes.

ss_server.py — takes command line args --nm-host --nm-port --ss-port --client-port --storage-dir; registers with NM and serves client connections for read/write/stream/undo.

client.py — CLI that prompts for username on start and supports the exact CLI commands described in the problem (VIEW, READ <file>, CREATE <file>, WRITE <file> <sentence_index>, ETIRW etc.). The client should:

send NM commands,

on SS returned address, connect and perform content operations,

print server responses in human readable form,

stream words with 0.1s delays in STREAM command.

run_test.sh (or a README test block) with a short scenario demonstrating: start NM, start one SS, start client as alice, create a file, write into it, start another client bob with read access, stream file, perform undo, show INFO, show VIEW -l. This script should run everything on localhost different ports.

Example CLI session (exact I/O)
Client prompt: >

User types:

> CREATE mouse.txt
Client sends to NM:

{"type":"create","username":"alice","filename":"mouse.txt"}
NM response:

{"status":"OK","op":"create","message":"File created","ss":{"host":"127.0.0.1","port":7001}}
Client prints:

File Created Successfully!
User types:

> WRITE mouse.txt 0
> 1 Im just a mouse.
> ETIRW
Flow:

Client asks NM for write token.

NM returns SS address and token.

Client connects to SS: begin_write -> updates -> end_write -> SS replies write successful.
Client prints:

Write Successful!
User:

> READ mouse.txt
Client prints file contents as returned by SS.

User:

> STREAM mouse.txt
Client prints each word with 0.1s delay as SS streams words.

Testing checklist (automatable)
 Start NM on port 8000

 Start SS on port 7001 with storage directory ss1_data/ and register to NM

 Start client as alice

 CREATE test.txt → success

 WRITE test.txt 0 multiple updates including sentence delimiter -> commit

 READ test.txt shows updated content

 UNDO test.txt reverts to previous version

 STREAM test.txt streams words with delay and tolerates SS disconnect mid-stream

 VIEW and VIEW -l show files and metadata

 INFO test.txt shows owner, timestamps, counts, ACL

 ADDACCESS -R test.txt bob then READ as bob succeeds

Implementation constraints for Copilot / GPT-5
Use only Python standard library (no external pip packages).

Use socket, threading or asyncio (either is fine). Keep code simple to fit in a single file each.

Keep everything cross-platform (Linux / macOS / Windows).

Comment tricky parts (sentence parsing, locking, atomic file replace).

Put a if __name__ == "__main__" block that parses CLI args and launches server/client.

Small README header to include at top of each file
what the file is

how to run python nm_server.py --port 8000

how to run python ss_server.py --nm-host 127.0.0.1 --nm-port 8000 --ss-port 7001 --client-port 7002 --storage-dir ./ss1_data

how to run python client.py --nm-host 127.0.0.1 --nm-port 8000 --client-port 6001 then it will prompt for username.

Anything optional but nice-to-have (bonus)
Simple replication: NM keeps two SS per file (primary + backup). On write, NM sends non-blocking replication command to backups.

Checkpoints: optional — save named checkpoints (but not required).

Pretty table output for VIEW -l (text table).

Small unit test function that runs a sequence of commands programmatically.

Final note to Copilot/GPT-5 (tone for the model)
Produce three standalone Python files (nm_server.py, ss_server.py, client.py) plus a short run_test.sh or README usage example. Make code robust but concise and well-commented. Prioritize correctness (locking, persistence, undo) and a clean, documented network protocol (JSON over TCP). The generated code should be directly runnable on a typical development machine and allow a quick end-to-end test sequence (create → write → read → undo → stream) without extra manual code edits.

If you want, I can now generate these three files and the test script for you. Do you want the full code now?

You said:
ALL IN C, AND MAKE SURE ALL THE BONUS IS ALSO DONE

ChatGPT said:
Prompt for VSCode Copilot / GPT-5 — Implement the mini NFS in C (NameServer + StorageServer + Client) — include ALL bonuses
Produce a complete, runnable C implementation (POSIX) of the described mini NFS: one nm_server (Name Server), one or more ss_server (Storage Servers), and a client CLI. Include build system (Makefile), any small third-party header/source you need (embed them in the repo), and a run_test.sh (or README with exact test steps). This prompt is tuned for a single-shot code generation: the model should output all files needed so a user can make and run end-to-end tests locally on Linux/macOS.

Hard constraints (must follow exactly):

All code in C (C11 or later) using POSIX sockets and threads (pthread). No external build-time dependencies beyond a standard C toolchain. If you need a JSON parser, include a tiny permissively licensed single-file parser (for example jsmn or a tiny custom parser) in the repo and use it — do not require libcurl, libjansson, etc. All such helper code must be provided as source files in the output.

Keep each major component in a single C source file with a matching header where useful: nm_server.c, ss_server.c, client.c. Also include common.h / common.c for shared types, error codes, logging helpers, and a small JSON utilities wrapper.

Provide a Makefile with targets: all, nm_server, ss_server, client, clean.

Provide a run_test.sh that launches NM, two SSes, then runs scripted client commands demonstrating all required features and bonuses.

Use newline-terminated JSON messages over TCP for RPC/messages. The prompt below defines the exact message formats (fields, required error codes, responses). Implement robust parsing and error handling.

Persist all metadata and files to disk as described below (use atomic rename() for file replaces).

Use pthread mutexes for thread-safety inside servers and implement sentence-level locking as described.

Use file I/O only from the standard library / POSIX; no DBs.

Provide comprehensive in-code comments explaining protocol, concurrency, persistence, and the bonus features.

What to implement (high-level)
Build an end-to-end system with these components and features (all required + ALL bonuses):

Name Server (nm_server)

Single instance. Holds persistent nm_state.json describing:

registered Storage Servers (SS id, host, nm_port, ss_client_port, last_heartbeat, file list),

file → primary SS mapping and replica list (at least primary + one backup),

users list and incoming access requests,

ACL master copy (owner, per-user RW/R),

an LRU cache for recent filename → SS lookups (in-memory; persist full mapping).

Accepts client registrations and NM-handled commands (VIEW, VIEW -a, VIEW -l, INFO, LIST, ADDACCESS, REMACCESS, REQUEST_ACCESS, APPROVE_ACCESS, DENY_ACCESS).

For CREATE/DELETE/EXEC/REQUEST_WRITE/UNDO it selects the primary SS (and schedules replication to backups).

Detects SS failures via heartbeats; on SS failure, reassigns primary/backup as needed and uses replicas to serve requests.

Implements efficient filename lookup: hashmap (average O(1)) plus a trie for prefix search (used for filtered VIEW).

Maintains logs (nm_log.txt) containing JSON lines with timestamps, IP/port, username, op, args, status, error code.

Storage Server (ss_server)

Registers with NM at startup (sends host, ports, storage directory, and list of files).

Stores files and metadata persistently on disk under storage_dir/ with structure:

storage_dir/
  files/<filename>.txt
  meta/<filename>.json
  undo/<filename>.bak
  checkpoints/<filename>_<tag>.bak
  log.txt
Metadata JSON includes owner, ACL, created, last_modified, last_accessed, word_count, char_count, sentence_count.

Supports direct client connections for READ, STREAM, WRITE sessions, and UNDO, and receives NM replication commands.

Sentence-level locking: maintain locks map (filename -> map<sentence_index, lock_info>). lock_info contains lock_id, username, timestamp, thread id. Use pthread_mutex_t to guard lock map updates.

WRITE session: client begins begin_write → SS atomically acquires lock for sentence(s) requested; updates via update messages; end_write commits (write to temp file and rename() atomically), store undo backup, update metadata (and send replication instructions to NM/backup SSes). Also support multiple sentence locks in same session (clients may lock different sentences).

UNDO: restore from undo/<filename>.bak and update metadata.

Checkpoints (bonus): support create/view/revert/list checkpoint commands as per spec. Store checkpoint files under checkpoints/.

Replication: when instructed (on create/write/delete), apply changes to local storage; support receiving replication commands from NM (sync file and metadata). On SS startup, perform resync if NM indicates it's a replica for certain files.

Logging: ss_log.txt JSON lines.

Client (client)

CLI program. On launch, prompts for username and registers to NM (register_client with client listen port info if needed). Implements interactive prompt > for commands:

VIEW [-a] [-l]

READ <filename>

CREATE <filename>

WRITE <filename> <sentence_index> (then accept lines: <word_index> <content> until ETIRW)

UNDO <filename>

INFO <filename>

DELETE <filename>

STREAM <filename>

LIST

ADDACCESS -R/-W <filename> <username>

REMACCESS <filename> <username>

EXEC <filename>

CHECKPOINT <filename> <tag> / VIEWCHECKPOINT <filename> <tag> / REVERT <filename> <tag> / LISTCHECKPOINTS <filename>

REQUEST_ACCESS <filename> (bonus) — NM stores request; owner can APPROVE_ACCESS or DENY_ACCESS.

For READ/STREAM/WRITE, client asks NM for SS host:port and a token. Client then connects directly to SS and performs the content operations using the SS protocol.

For STREAM, display words one by one with 0.1s delay. If SS disconnects mid-stream, print clear error message.

For EXEC, NM fetches file from SS and executes commands on NM (subprocess) and returns output to client (NM must pipe stdout+stderr back).

Replication & Fault Tolerance (BONUS — must be implemented)

NM maintains primary + backup(s) per file.

On CREATE/WRITE/DELETE operations NM instructs backups asynchronously (fire-and-forget) to apply same operation. NM must not wait for backups to ack writes (asynchronous replication), but must log replication requests.

On SS failure detection, NM promotes backups to primary and reassigns replication targets.

Implement SS heartbeat to NM so NM can detect down SSes. SSs try to reconnect on network disconnect.

When a down SS comes back online, NM instructs it to resync files it should host (matching replica lists).

Checkpoints (BONUS — must be implemented)

Users can create named checkpoints: CHECKPOINT <filename> <checkpoint_tag>; SS stores checkpoints/<filename>_<tag>.bak.

VIEWCHECKPOINT, REVERT, LISTCHECKPOINTS commands supported. NM coordinates where to fetch checkpoints if primary not available.

Requesting Access (BONUS — must be implemented)

REQUEST_ACCESS <filename> — client sends to NM; NM records request under file owner’s pending requests.

Owner can VIEWREQUESTS <filename> and APPROVE_ACCESS <filename> <username> -R/-W or DENY_ACCESS.

NM sends notifications only by storing state (no push notifications needed); owner sees requests when they query.

Other bonus: Unique Factor

Provide a small web of statistics printed by NM on demand: number of files, number of active locks, replication lag (count of pending replication jobs). (This is optional but recommended.)

Network / Message formats (exact; JSON newline-terminated)
All messages are JSON objects followed by \n. Implement minimal JSON parsing; if using jsmn include it in repo.

Error codes (required — use these exact strings):
ERR_NO_SUCH_FILE, ERR_NO_ACCESS, ERR_LOCKED, ERR_INVALID_INDEX, ERR_SS_DOWN, ERR_UNDO_EMPTY, ERR_ALREADY_EXISTS, ERR_INVALID_CMD, ERR_INTERNAL, ERR_NOT_OWNER, ERR_ALREADY_REQUESTED

Client → NM messages (examples)
Register client:

{"type":"register_client","username":"alice","client_port":6001}
VIEW:

{"type":"view","username":"alice","flags":"-al"}
READ (NM returns SS address and token):

{"type":"read","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt"}
CREATE:

{"type":"create","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt"}
REQUEST_WRITE (NM returns SS + token for client's direct SS connection):

{"type":"request_write","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt","sentence_index":1}
INFO, DELETE, LIST, ADDACCESS, REMACCESS, UNDO, EXEC, CHECKPOINT, VIEWCHECKPOINT, REVERT, LISTCHECKPOINTS, REQUEST_ACCESS, VIEWREQUESTS, APPROVE_ACCESS, DENY_ACCESS follow analogous JSON fields.

NM → Client responses (uniform)
Success:

{"status":"OK","op":"create","payload":{...}}
Error:

{"status":"ERR","code":"ERR_NO_ACCESS","message":"User alice has no write access to foo.txt"}
NM → Client for SS-backed ops (READ/WRITE/STREAM)
{"status":"OK","op":"request_write","ss":{"host":"127.0.0.1","port":7001},"token":"abc123","replicas":[{"host":"127.0.0.1","port":7002}]}
Client ↔ SS messages (after NM gives SS address and token)
Client connects to SS and sends JSON messages similar to:

Begin read:

{"type":"read","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt","token":"abc123"}
Begin stream:

{"type":"stream","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt","token":"abc123"}
SS for stream will send repeated:

{"type":"word","word":"Hello"}
then:

{"type":"stop"}
Begin write:

{"type":"begin_write","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt","sentence_indices":[1,2],"token":"abc123"}
SS replies:

{"status":"OK","lock_id":"L123"}
or error if locked:

{"status":"ERR","code":"ERR_LOCKED","message":"Sentence locked"}
Update:

{"type":"update","lock_id":"L123","word_index":4,"content":"newword"}
SS replies {"status":"OK"} on success.

End write:

{"type":"end_write","lock_id":"L123","commit":true}
SS commits, stores undo, logs, and replies:

{"status":"OK","message":"Write Successful"}
Undo:

{"type":"undo","username":"alice","filename":"foo.txt","token":"abc123"}
SS replies {"status":"OK","message":"Undo Successful"} or ERR_UNDO_EMPTY.

Replication command (NM→SS; SS should accept):

{"type":"replicate","op":"write","filename":"foo.txt","tempfile_path":"/tmp/....","meta":{...}}
SS applies replication.

On-disk layout (exact)
Storage Server root storage_dir/

storage_dir/
  files/<filename>.txt           # canonical file content (text)
  meta/<filename>.json           # metadata JSON (owner,acl,created,last_modified,last_accessed,word_count,char_count,sentence_count)
  undo/<filename>.bak            # previous committed text (for one-level undo)
  checkpoints/<filename>_<tag>.bak  # checkpoints
  ss_state.json                  # ss-level state (list of files, last sync time, pending replication jobs)
  ss_log.txt                     # JSON-lines logs
Name Server state

nm_state.json    # includes file->primary+replica list, SS registry, users, pending requests
nm_log.txt       # JSON-lines logs
Sentence parsing rules (exact algorithm to implement in C)
A sentence ends at any . ! or ?. Every such character is a delimiter even within abbreviations like e.g.. The sentence includes the delimiter.

Parse by scanning file bytes: accumulate chars until a delimiter is encountered, append delimiter to sentence, then trim leading/trailing spaces on sentence extraction for words.

Words in a sentence: split by single ' ' (space). Consecutive spaces should result in ignoring empty tokens (i.e., treat runs of spaces as single separators).

Sentence indices are 0-based.

When a WRITE commit changes sentence boundaries (because content contains delimiters), re-parse file and update sentence indices.

Concurrency & locking (exact)
Server must support multiple client connections (multi-threaded).

Use pthread_mutex_t for global SS data structures; use fine-grained locks for per-file lock maps.

begin_write acquires locks for specified sentence indices atomically (fail if any requested sentence already locked).

Locks persist until end_write or client disconnect (then release locks and rollback uncommitted changes).

Implement lock timeouts (e.g., 5 minutes) — expired locks are released automatically.

Persistence & atomic updates
For commit: write to files/.tmp_<filename>, then fsync() the file (if possible) and rename() to files/<filename>.txt to ensure atomic swap.

Update undo/<filename>.bak by moving previous file content there before rename.

Update meta/<filename>.json atomically (write to temp then rename).

Logging & Error Handling
Every operation logged as JSON-line with: timestamp (ISO8601), local IP/port, username, op, args, result status, error code (if any).

NM and SS share a log_json(...) helper in common.c.

Performance requirements
NM must use a hashmap for filename→SS mapping (average O(1)). Also implement a simple trie for prefix search (used by VIEW -a with prefix filters). Implement an in-memory LRU cache for most recent 100 lookups.

NM should persist nm_state.json periodically and on each mapping change.

Testing & run script
Provide run_test.sh that:

Builds the binaries.

Creates directories nm_data/ ss1_data/ ss2_data/.

Starts nm_server on port 8000.

Starts ss_server #1 on ports 7001 (nm link) and 7002 (client link) with storage ss1_data.

Starts ss_server #2 on ports 7003 (nm link) and 7004 (client link) with storage ss2_data.

Runs scripted client sessions (non-interactive) or multiple clients in background demonstrating:

CREATE test.txt as alice (NM selects SS1 as primary, SS2 as backup).

WRITE test.txt 0 with multiple updates including delimiters; commit.

READ test.txt as bob (after owner gave read access) using SS direct connection.

STREAM test.txt and simulate ss2 crash mid-stream to show error handling (kill ss2 process).

UNDO test.txt revert to previous version.

CHECKPOINT test.txt v1, LISTCHECKPOINTS, VIEWCHECKPOINT test.txt v1, REVERT test.txt v1.

REQUEST_ACCESS test.txt by a user and APPROVE_ACCESS by owner.

Demonstrate replication: show that primary's write triggered replication to backup (check backup meta file).

Bring down primary SS and show NM fails over to backup (promote to primary) and client operations continue.

The script should print clear PASS/FAIL markers for each step.

Deliverables (exact files to output)
nm_server.c (+ optional nm_server.h)

ss_server.c (+ optional ss_server.h)

client.c

common.h, common.c (shared code: logging, JSON helper wrappers, error codes)

jsmn.h and jsmn.c (or similar tiny JSON parser included)

Makefile

run_test.sh

README.md describing how to build and run, and the example interactive session.

Implementation constraints & style
Use clear, idiomatic C, modularized with functions. Keep source files under ~2000 lines each if possible.

Strong comments for tricky areas: socket framing, JSON parsing, sentence parsing, locking, replication flows, failure detection/resync.

Use select()/poll() or spawn threads per client (thread-per-connection acceptable).

Avoid global unsynchronized state; protect shared structures with mutexes.

Keep command parsing robust: validate indices (non-negative and within bounds), permissions, lock availability.

On unexpected runtime errors, log them and send ERR_INTERNAL with a helpful message to the client.

Example of exact CLI flow and messages (must match implemented behavior)
Client types:

> CREATE mouse.txt
Client sends to NM:

{"type":"create","username":"alice","filename":"mouse.txt"}
NM replies:

{"status":"OK","op":"create","payload":{"ss":{"host":"127.0.0.1","port":7002},"replicas":[{"host":"127.0.0.1","port":7004}]}}
Client prints:

File Created Successfully!
Write flow:

> WRITE mouse.txt 0
(Client asks NM for token)
(NM returns SS host:port + token)
(Client connects to SS)
Client -> SS:
{"type":"begin_write","username":"alice","filename":"mouse.txt","sentence_indices":[0],"token":"abc123"}
SS -> Client:
{"status":"OK","lock_id":"L123"}
Client -> SS:
{"type":"update","lock_id":"L123","word_index":1,"content":"Im just a mouse."}
...
{"type":"end_write","lock_id":"L123","commit":true}
SS -> Client:
{"status":"OK","message":"Write Successful"}
Final note to the code generator
Produce complete C source files and the Makefile and run_test.sh in your response. The goal is to be able to make and run ./nm_server --port 8000, ./ss_server --nm-host 127.0.0.1 --nm-port 8000 --ss-port 7002 --client-port 7003 --storage-dir ./ss1_data, then start client to exercise the system. Include helpful inline README comments at the top of each file explaining usage.

If you cannot implement every single bonus item perfectly (e.g., full automatic failover with state resync), implement them in a best-effort robust way and clearly document limitations in the README. But do implement replication, checkpoints, and request-access functionality as described.

---

## One-shot prompt for VS Code Copilot/GPT-5 (C, POSIX, fits current repo)

You are generating code to complete and extend an existing C implementation of a distributed document system (NameServer NM, StorageServer SS, and Client) in this repo. Implement all core requirements and ALL bonuses as described below, strictly following the project’s existing architecture, files, and network protocol.

Important constraints:
- Language: C (C11). POSIX sockets and pthreads only; no external libraries.
- Protocol: length-prefixed frames (uint32 big-endian) with minimal JSON payloads. Use the existing helpers in `common/net_proto.c/h`. Do NOT switch to newline-delimited JSON.
- Keep the current directory layout and file names. Modify/extend the following files only, adding small new .c/.h files only when necessary:
  - `nm_main.c`, `nm/nm_persist.c/h`, `nm/nm_dir.c/h`
  - `ss_main.c`, `ss/ss_tokenize.c/h`
  - `cli_main.c`
  - `common/net_proto.c/h`, `common/tickets.c/h` (only if needed)
  - Add `nm/nm_replication.c/h` and `nm/nm_heartbeat.c/h` if you prefer to organize new NM logic.
  - Add `README.md` updates and `run_test.sh` to validate everything.
- Error status strings must match the existing style returned to the client: "OK", "ERR_BADREQ", "ERR_NOTFOUND", "ERR_NOAUTH", "ERR_CONFLICT", "ERR_LOCKED", "ERR_UNAVAILABLE", "ERR_INTERNAL".

Deliverables:
- Extend current code to fully support all required client commands and all bonus features end-to-end.
- Update the Makefile if needed (but keep current targets working).
- Provide a `run_test.sh` that builds and runs NM + two SS + scripted client flows demonstrating all features (core + bonus).

What to implement and where

1) Protocol and framing (unchanged; align to current code)
- Use length-prefixed messages via `send_msg`/`recv_msg`.
- Message “type” fields are uppercase and already used: e.g., "LOOKUP", "READ", "BEGIN_WRITE", "APPLY", "END_WRITE", "UNDO", "INFO", "STREAM", "CREATE", "DELETE", "RENAME", "PUT", "LIST_USERS", "VIEW", "EXEC", "CREATEFOLDER", "VIEWFOLDER", "MOVE", "CHECKPOINT", "LISTCHECKPOINTS", "VIEWCHECKPOINT", "HISTORY", "REVERT".
- Keep using tickets for SS authorization via `ticket_build`/`ticket_validate`.

2) Complete core features (fill gaps; reuse existing behavior)
- Sentence-level locking (already present in `ss_main.c`): keep as is, ensure lock is released on disconnect and at END_WRITE; return "ERR_LOCKED" when busy.
- Undo (already present; single level): Keep as is.
- INFO (already present): Keep count calculations and return JSON fields: size, words, chars, mtime, atime.
- VIEW/VIEW -l (already implemented via NM): Keep behavior.
- Access control (already in `nm_persist.c` ACL): Enforce at NM for LOOKUP/INFO/VIEW -l/EXEC and at SS with tickets (keep consistent with how tickets encode op).

3) Bonus: Replication and fault tolerance (implement fully)
Goal: Primary + backup SS for each file. Asynchronous replication; NM detects SS failure and fails over to a replica; when SS returns, NM resyncs.

A) NM persistent state changes (in `nm/nm_persist.c/h`)
- Extend state to store replicas: for each filename, store primary ssId and a list of replica ssIds (at least one).
  - Add functions:
    - `int nm_state_set_replicas(const char *file, const int *replicas, size_t n);`
    - `size_t nm_state_get_replicas(const char *file, int *out, size_t max);`
    - `int nm_state_get_primary(const char *file, int *out_ssid);` (wrap existing dir primary)
    - Update on save/load in `nm_state_save`/`nm_state_load` (extend JSON schema; keep backward compatible by treating missing replicas as empty).
- Keep existing `directory` mapping as the primary.

B) NM heartbeat tracking (new in `nm_main.c` or `nm/nm_heartbeat.c`)
- Maintain `last_heartbeat` per SS id. Add in-memory registry: ssId → {ctrl_port, data_port, last_heartbeat, is_up}.
- New NM endpoint types:
  - "SS_HEARTBEAT": payload: {"type":"SS_HEARTBEAT","ssId":<id>}. NM updates last_heartbeat for the SS and marks it up.
- Implement a background thread in NM that:
  - Periodically checks last_heartbeat (e.g., every 2s).
  - If a server is silent for > 6s, mark it down.
  - For each file whose primary is down, promote a live replica as the new primary in `nm_state`. Keep any other live replicas as replicas.
  - Save state after promotions.
- When a down SS comes back (receives heartbeat or a new register), NM marks it up and resyncs (see D).

C) Replication orchestration at NM (new in `nm_main.c`, optionally `nm_replication.c`)
- On CREATE/DELETE/RENAME performed by NM on primary SS today, also schedule asynchronous replication commands to all replicas. Do not block the client; fire-and-forget.
  - CREATE: On primary OK, issue CREATE on each replica.
  - DELETE: On primary OK, issue DELETE on each replica.
  - RENAME/MOVE: On primary OK, issue same RENAME on each replica; update ACL mapping and NM state accordingly.
- On WRITE commits: Primary SS notifies NM (see E). NM then fetches the current content of the file from the primary (using a READ ticket as NM) and sends a "PUT" with the full body to each replica SS asynchronously.

D) Resync on SS recovery (NM-driven)
- When a SS transitions from down→up (via heartbeat/register), NM scans files that should have replicas on this SS and pushes the current primary version to it using "PUT" so it becomes consistent. Then add it to the file’s replica set (if not already).

E) SS commit notification to NM (new in `ss_main.c`)
- After successful END_WRITE commit, SS must send a one-shot message to NM to trigger replication:
  - SS opens a short-lived connection to NM (same host/port SS used for register).
  - Sends {"type":"SS_COMMIT","file":"<name>","ssId":<id>}.
  - NM verifies mapping: if the notifying SS is the current primary for that file, it schedules asynchronous replication to replicas (as in C).
  - Idempotence: if multiple notifications arrive, NM should be robust (last write wins).
- Also implement periodic heartbeat from SS:
  - A detached thread in SS sends "SS_HEARTBEAT" to NM every 2 seconds.

F) LOOKUP with failover
- Update NM’s LOOKUP handling to:
  - Return the primary SS data port and ticket.
  - If primary is marked down, use a live replica instead (and optionally swap primary mapping immediately).
  - Return "ERR_UNAVAILABLE" only if no live SS (primary or any replica) is available.

4) Bonus: Checkpoints (already implemented)
- Already exists: CHECKPOINT, LISTCHECKPOINTS, VIEWCHECKPOINT, REVERT by name.
- Keep as is; ensure tickets for VIEWCHECKPOINT/LISTCHECKPOINTS/REVERT are checked (already done).

5) Bonus: Hierarchical folders (already implemented)
- Keep CREATEFOLDER/VIEWFOLDER/MOVE behaviors as coded. Ensure replication also covers RENAME/MOVE across replicas for all impacted files (covered in 3C).

6) Bonus: Requesting Access (implement)
Add these NM-handled commands (no SS contact needed):
- REQUEST_ACCESS <filename>
  - Client → NM: {"type":"REQUEST_ACCESS","file":"...","user":"reqUser"}
  - NM stores a pending request under the file’s owner for that specific file.
  - Response: {"status":"OK"} or "ERR_NOTFOUND" if file missing.
- VIEWREQUESTS <filename>
  - Only owner can view. Returns list of pending usernames.
  - Response: {"status":"OK","requests":["user2","user3"]}
- APPROVE_ACCESS <filename> <username> -R|-W
  - Only owner. NM updates ACL (grant R or RW) and removes the request.
  - Response: {"status":"OK"}
- DENY_ACCESS <filename> <username>
  - Only owner. Remove the request.
  - Response: {"status":"OK"}
Persistence:
- Extend `nm_state_save/load` JSON with a "requests" object:
  - {"requests":{"<file>":["userA","userB"]}}.
- Add helper functions in `nm_persist.c/h`:
  - add/list/remove requests per file; owner check can reuse existing `nm_acl_get_owner`.

Update client (`cli_main.c`):
- Add commands:
  - REQUEST_ACCESS <file>
  - VIEWREQUESTS <file>
  - APPROVE_ACCESS <file> -R|-W <user>
  - DENY_ACCESS <file> <user>
- Format responses simply (OK or list).

7) Bonus: Unique Factor (simple stats)
Add an NM command "STATS" (client can invoke "STATS"):
- NM returns counts: {"status":"OK","files":N,"activeLocks":M,"replicationQueue":Q}
  - files: number of mapped files (from nm_state).
  - activeLocks: pull a best-effort total from SS? To keep it simple, maintain an NM-side counter of "inflight replications" and set activeLocks to -1 if not tracked. Alternatively, NM can ask all SS via a one-shot "SS_LOCKS" request on data port, but a stub is acceptable if documented.
  - replicationQueue: number of pending async replication tasks not yet attempted or in-flight threads.

8) Logging (lightweight)
- Leave existing stderr prints. Optionally add JSON-line logs:
  - NM: `nm_log.txt` lines: {"ts":...,"op":"LOOKUP","user":"...","file":"...","status":"OK"...}
  - SS: `ss_data/log.txt` similar lines.
- Not mandatory to be exhaustive, but do add logs for replication and failover events.

9) Tickets (unchanged)
- Keep tickets as-is. For replication PUT/RENAME/DELETE from NM to replicas, NM acts as trusted; replicate via direct calls to SS endpoints that don’t require tickets (i.e., current SS accepts PUT/RENAME/DELETE without ticket for NM-initiated replication). If you prefer uniformity, NM can build tickets with op "REPL" and SS can accept those, but simpler is to allow unauthenticated PUT/RENAME/DELETE only from NM; you may reuse loopback-only assumption (127.0.0.1) as per current code.

10) Client I/O (keep existing UX)
- Do not change current prompts/flows. Ensure:
  - VIEW [-a] [-l]
  - READ <file>
  - CREATE <file>
  - WRITE <file> <sentenceIndex> then interactive APPLY … ETIRW
  - UNDO <file>
  - INFO <file>
  - DELETE <file>
  - STREAM <file>
  - LIST
  - ADDACCESS -R|-W <file> <user>
  - REMACCESS <file> <user>
  - EXEC <file>
  - mkdir/lsf/mv (mapped to CREATEFOLDER/VIEWFOLDER/MOVE)
  - history/revert (version) and checkpoint commands as already present
  - New: REQUEST_ACCESS, VIEWREQUESTS, APPROVE_ACCESS, DENY_ACCESS, STATS

11) Exact new/updated message formats
- SS → NM (write commit notify):
  - {"type":"SS_COMMIT","file":"<name>","ssId":<id>}
  - NM response: {"status":"OK"}.
- SS → NM (heartbeat):
  - {"type":"SS_HEARTBEAT","ssId":<id>}
  - NM response: {"status":"OK"}.
- Client → NM (request access):
  - {"type":"REQUEST_ACCESS","file":"...","user":"..."} → {"status":"OK"|"ERR_NOTFOUND"|"ERR_ALREADY_REQUESTED"}.
- Client → NM (view requests):
  - {"type":"VIEWREQUESTS","file":"...","user":"..."} → {"status":"OK","requests":["u1","u2"]} or "ERR_NOAUTH".
- Client → NM (approve/deny):
  - {"type":"APPROVE_ACCESS","file":"...","user":"owner","target":"u2","mode":"R|W|RW"}
  - {"type":"DENY_ACCESS","file":"...","user":"owner","target":"u2"}
  - Responses: {"status":"OK"} or relevant ERR.
- Client → NM (stats):
  - {"type":"STATS"} → {"status":"OK","files":N,"activeLocks":M,"replicationQueue":Q}.
- NM → Client for LOOKUP (unchanged):
  - {"status":"OK","ssAddr":"127.0.0.1","ssDataPort":<port>,"ticket":"..."}.
- NM → SS replication commands (use existing SS handlers):
  - CREATE: {"type":"CREATE","file":"..."}
  - DELETE: {"type":"DELETE","file":"..."}
  - RENAME: {"type":"RENAME","file":"old","newFile":"new"}
  - PUT: {"type":"PUT","file":"...","body":"<full text>"}

12) Persistence files and on-disk layout (keep current)
- SS keeps:
  - ss_data/files/<file>
  - ss_data/undo/<file>.undo
  - ss_data/history/<file>.<n>.snap
  - ss_data/checkpoints/<file>/<tag>.chk
- NM keeps: `nm_state.json`, extended to include replicas and access requests.
  - Ensure backward compatibility if file already exists: missing fields default sensibly.

13) Build and run
- Keep Makefile working; adjust if adding .c/.h files.
- Provide `run_test.sh` that:
  - Builds the project.
  - Starts NM on 5000.
  - Starts SS1 (ctrl 6001, data 6002, id 1) and SS2 (ctrl 6003, data 6004, id 2).
  - Demonstrates:
    - Client as alice: CREATE test.txt → primary chosen; verify VIEW and INFO.
    - WRITE test.txt 0 with multiple APPLY updates including sentence delimiters; END_WRITE; READ shows updated content.
    - Replication: after commit, verify SS2 has the replicated content (by NM PUT).
    - STREAM test.txt, and simulate replica down (kill SS2) mid-stream from SS2 only; client shows error if connected to down SS; then retry via primary.
    - UNDO test.txt restores previous version.
    - CHECKPOINT test.txt v1; LISTCHECKPOINTS; VIEWCHECKPOINT; REVERT by name.
    - REQUEST_ACCESS test.txt by bob; as alice, VIEWREQUESTS; APPROVE_ACCESS -R bob; as bob, READ succeeds; APPROVE_ACCESS -W bob; WRITE succeeds.
    - Failover: kill primary SS1; NM promotes SS2; READ/WRITE continue (possibly with a warning); bring SS1 back; NM resyncs SS1 from SS2 and re-adds it as replica.
    - STATS prints nonzero metrics; replicationQueue drains to 0.
  - Print PASS/FAIL lines.

Acceptance criteria
- All commands return correct JSON responses with the exact status strings used elsewhere.
- Replication is asynchronous and does not block client responses.
- Heartbeats detect SS downtime; NM promotes a replica automatically.
- On SS restart, NM resyncs file contents to it and re-adds it as backup.
- Request Access flow works and is persisted.
- Existing client commands remain backward compatible.
- `run_test.sh` runs on Linux with bash, a standard C toolchain, and no extra dependencies.

Notes and assumptions
- Keep networking to localhost (127.0.0.1). Security is out of scope; NM to SS replication calls may be unauthenticated.
- The minimal JSON helpers in `net_proto.c` are intentionally simple; construct/parse fields carefully and keep strings short to avoid overflow.
- Keep code style and structure consistent with the repo.

Please implement the above by editing the specified files directly, preserving the existing protocol and CLI UX. Add any small helpers you need, but avoid large refactors. Make sure to run your own end-to-end test in `run_test.sh` and ensure the build passes.

If you encounter missing details, prefer a minimal, consistent approach aligned with the current code.

---
